title: Health Literacy Consistency Baseline Evaluation
key_findings: Response length, preference expression, and decision activeness show significant differentiation by HL level (p<0.001) — model partially sensitive to HL setting in profile.
Question-asking is universally low across all conditions (<3 per dialogue vs literature norm of 4-9). The fixed clinician prompt set may suppress patient questions.
Pretend understanding (unqualified affirmation) never occurs. The LLM never produces this hallmark low-HL behavior regardless of strategy.
Literacy-anchored prompting improves low-HL alignment on response length (25→18 words), question count (2.8→1.2), and decision passivity (-0.2→-1.0).
Lexical diversity moves in the wrong direction under literacy-anchored (0.56→0.63 for low HL). Low-HL patients should have lower, not higher lexical diversity.
Most per-dimension improvements are not statistically significant due to small sample size (only 5 low-HL profiles).
Three existing strategies (standard/consistency/structured_state) show minimal differentiation — composite scores differ by <0.02.
limitations: Only 5 low-HL profiles — insufficient statistical power for per-HL analysis.
Fixed clinician prompt set may suppress patient-initiated behaviors (esp. question-asking).
Composite scoring may overstate consistency (combines well-predicted and poorly-predicted dimensions).
next_steps: Generate 3 additional low-HL profiles to balance the dataset (8/8/8).
